expanded_description: Understanding why certain elements, particularly chromium and copper, have electron configurations that differ from the expected Aufbau principle predictions, including the stability of half-filled and fully-filled d subshells and other transition metal exceptions.

core_concept.principle: Some elements have electron configurations that deviate from the expected Aufbau order because half-filled ($d^5$) and fully-filled ($d^{10}$) d subshells provide extra stability through exchange energy and symmetric electron distribution.
core_concept.explanation: While the Aufbau principle predicts electron configurations for most elements, chromium (Cr, Z=24) and copper (Cu, Z=29) are notable exceptions. Chromium is [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$ instead of [Ar] $4s^2 3d^4$, and copper is [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$ instead of [Ar] $4s^2 3d^9$. This occurs because the energy gained from having a half-filled or fully-filled d subshell (due to exchange energy and reduced electron-electron repulsion) exceeds the energy cost of promoting an electron from 4s to 3d. Similar exceptions occur in other transition metals, particularly in periods 5 and 6.

image.alt: Diagram comparing expected vs. actual electron configurations for Cr and Cu showing electron promotion from 4s to 3d
image.caption: Cr and Cu promote one 4s electron to 3d to achieve half-filled ($d^5$) or fully-filled ($d^{10}$) subshells for extra stability

sections.section_title: Why Exceptions Occur: Stability of d Subshells
sections.introduction: The Aufbau principle works well for most elements, but certain transition metals have configurations that seem to violate the expected filling order. These exceptions are not random errors but reflect fundamental quantum mechanical principles about electron stability, exchange energy, and subshell symmetry.

key_concept.definition: Electron configuration exceptions occur when the energy gained from achieving a half-filled ($d^5$) or fully-filled ($d^{10}$) d subshell exceeds the energy cost of promoting an electron from the s orbital to the d orbital.
key_concept.context: These exceptions are most common in transition metals where 4s and 3d orbitals are very close in energy, making electron promotion energetically favorable when it creates symmetric d subshell configurations.

principles.statement: Electrons with parallel spins in degenerate orbitals experience exchange energy stabilization, a quantum mechanical effect that lowers the total energy of the atom.
principles.application: Half-filled subshells ($d^5$) maximize the number of parallel spins, maximizing exchange energy stabilization and making these configurations more stable than expected.
principles.example: Cr: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$ has 6 unpaired electrons (5 in 3d, 1 in 4s) with parallel spins, maximizing exchange energy
principles.analogy: Like people standing in a circle holding hands: more connections (parallel spins) = more stable arrangement.

principles.statement: Half-filled and fully-filled subshells have symmetric electron distributions that minimize electron-electron repulsion.
principles.application: Symmetric distributions are more stable than asymmetric ones; $d^5$ and $d^{10}$ configurations achieve maximum symmetry.
principles.example: Cu: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$ has fully-filled 3d subshell with symmetric charge distribution
principles.analogy: Like a perfectly balanced mobile: symmetric arrangements are more stable than lopsided ones.

principles.statement: The energy difference between 4s and 3d orbitals is very small in transition metals, making electron promotion energetically feasible.
principles.application: When 4s and 3d are nearly degenerate, the stability gained from half-filled or fully-filled d subshells can overcome the promotion energy cost.
principles.example: In Cr and Cu, 4s-3d energy gap is small enough that promoting one 4s electron to 3d is energetically favorable
principles.analogy: Like moving between two nearly-equal floors in a building: small effort to move, but big benefit in room arrangement.

classifications.value: Elements with 5 d electrons achieve extra stability from half-filled d subshell
classifications.behavior: One s electron promotes to d to achieve $d^5$ configuration

examples.explanation: Expected: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^4$; Actual: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$; gains stability from half-filled 3d
Expected: [Kr] $5s^2 4d^4$; Actual: [Kr] $5s^1 4d^5$; same pattern as Cr in period 5

classifications.value: Elements with 10 d electrons achieve extra stability from fully-filled d subshell
characteristics: Complete d subshell with all orbitals filled
classifications.behavior: One s electron promotes to d to achieve $d^{10}$ configuration

examples.explanation: Expected: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^9$; Actual: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$; gains stability from fully-filled 3d
Expected: [Kr] $5s^2 4d^9$; Actual: [Kr] $5s^1 4d^{10}$; same pattern as Cu in period 5
Expected: [Xe] $6s^2 5d^9$; Actual: [Xe] $6s^1 5d^{10}$; same pattern in period 6

classifications.value: The energy gained from subshell stability must exceed the promotion energy cost
characteristics: 4s-3d energy gap ~2-3 eV in transition metals
Exchange energy gain ~1-2 eV for $d^5$ or $d^{10}$
Net energy change determines whether exception occurs
classifications.behavior: Exception occurs when: Exchange energy + Symmetry energy > Promotion energy

examples.explanation: Promotion cost: ~2 eV; Exchange energy gain: ~3 eV; Net: favorable by ~1 eV
For elements like Ti ($d^2$) or V ($d^3$), exchange energy gain doesn't exceed promotion cost

worked_examples.title: Predicting Chromium Configuration
worked_examples.problem: Predict the electron configuration for chromium (Z = 24) and explain why it differs from the expected Aufbau configuration.

step_by_step.explanation: Following Aufbau: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^4$ (24 electrons total)
Expected 3d⁴ has 4 electrons in 5 orbitals; not half-filled or fully-filled
Promote one 4s electron to 3d to achieve 3d⁵
3d⁵ is half-filled: 5 unpaired electrons with parallel spins; maximum exchange energy
Energy gain from half-filled 3d exceeds promotion cost

worked_examples.final_answer: Chromium: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$; exception occurs because half-filled 3d⁵ subshell provides extra stability through exchange energy and symmetric distribution
worked_examples.concept_applied: Half-filled d subshells ($d^5$) are particularly stable; one 4s electron promotes to 3d to achieve this configuration.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Copper Configuration
worked_examples.problem: Predict the electron configuration for copper (Z = 29) and explain the exception.

step_by_step.explanation: Following Aufbau: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^9$ (29 electrons total)
Expected 3d⁹ has 9 electrons in 5 orbitals; one electron short of fully-filled
Promote one 4s electron to 3d to achieve 3d¹⁰
3d¹⁰ is fully-filled: all orbitals complete; maximum symmetry and minimum repulsion
Energy gain from fully-filled 3d exceeds promotion cost

worked_examples.final_answer: Copper: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$; exception occurs because fully-filled 3d¹⁰ subshell provides extra stability through symmetry and reduced electron repulsion
worked_examples.concept_applied: Fully-filled d subshells ($d^{10}$) are particularly stable; one 4s electron promotes to 3d to achieve this configuration.

sections.section_title: Chromium and Copper: The Classic Exceptions
sections.key_concept: Chromium (Group 6) and copper (Group 11) are the most well-known electron configuration exceptions, serving as the primary examples of half-filled and fully-filled d subshell stability in introductory chemistry.

categories.description: Elements in Group 6 often show $s^1 d^5$ configurations to achieve half-filled d subshells.
categories.detailed_mechanism: The exchange energy from 5 parallel d electrons plus the symmetric distribution outweighs the energy cost of having only one s electron.

examples.explanation: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$; 6 unpaired electrons total; paramagnetic
[Kr] $5s^1 4d^5$; same pattern as Cr in period 5
[Xe] $6s^2 4f^{14} 5d^4$; does NOT follow exception (6s-5d gap larger in period 6)

categories.description: Elements in Group 11 often show $s^1 d^{10}$ configurations to achieve fully-filled d subshells.
categories.detailed_mechanism: The stability from a complete d subshell with symmetric charge distribution outweighs the energy cost of having only one s electron.

examples.explanation: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$; 1 unpaired electron; paramagnetic
[Kr] $5s^1 4d^{10}$; same pattern as Cu in period 5
[Xe] $6s^1 5d^{10}$; same pattern in period 6 (relativistic effects also contribute)

categories.description: Exception configurations affect magnetic properties due to different numbers of unpaired electrons.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Unpaired electrons create magnetic moments; exception configurations often have more unpaired electrons than expected.

examples.explanation: Expected $4s^2 3d^4$: 4 unpaired; Actual $4s^1 3d^5$: 6 unpaired; more paramagnetic than expected
Expected $4s^2 3d^9$: 1 unpaired; Actual $4s^1 3d^{10}$: 1 unpaired; same number but different orbital

categories.description: Exception configurations influence oxidation states and chemical reactivity.
categories.detailed_mechanism: The stability of half-filled or fully-filled d subshells affects which oxidation states are most common and stable.

examples.explanation: Cr²⁺: [Ar] $3d^4$; Cr³⁺: [Ar] $3d^3$; Cr⁶⁺: [Ar] (no d electrons); +3 most stable
Cu⁺: [Ar] $3d^{10}$ (fully-filled, stable); Cu²⁺: [Ar] $3d^9$ (common in compounds)

classifications.value: Exception configurations still have the correct total number of electrons
characteristics: Cr: 24 electrons total (18 from Ar + 1 from 4s + 5 from 3d)
Cu: 29 electrons total (18 from Ar + 1 from 4s + 10 from 3d)
No electrons are created or destroyed, only redistributed
classifications.behavior: Always verify total electron count equals atomic number for neutral atoms

classifications.value: 4s and 3d orbitals are very close in energy for transition metals
characteristics: Energy gap ~2-3 eV in period 4 transition metals
4s electrons removed before 3d when forming cations
Small gap allows electron promotion when energetically favorable
classifications.behavior: Close energy spacing enables exceptions; larger gaps in other elements prevent exceptions

examples.explanation: Ca: [Ar] $4s^2$; no 3d electrons; 4s-3d gap too large for promotion
Zn: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^{10}$; already fully-filled; no benefit from promotion

classifications.value: Exceptions occur in specific groups and periods where energy conditions are favorable
characteristics: Most common in periods 4 and 5 transition metals
Group 6 (Cr, Mo) and Group 11 (Cu, Ag, Au) most consistent
Period 6 has more exceptions due to f orbital effects
Not all elements in these groups show exceptions
classifications.behavior: Exception patterns reflect periodic trends in orbital energies and electron interactions

examples.explanation: Cr and Cu show exceptions; other period 4 transition metals follow Aufbau
Mo, Ag show exceptions; Nb, Ru, Rh, Pd also have exceptions (more complex)

worked_examples.title: Comparing Expected vs. Actual Configurations
worked_examples.problem: For chromium and copper, write both the expected Aufbau configuration and the actual configuration. Explain the difference in terms of stability.

step_by_step.explanation: Following Aufbau order: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^4$
One 4s electron promotes to 3d for half-filled stability
Following Aufbau order: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^9$
One 4s electron promotes to 3d for fully-filled stability
Cr: $d^5$ half-filled maximizes exchange energy; Cu: $d^{10}$ fully-filled maximizes symmetry
step_by_step.calculation: Energy gain from subshell stability > promotion energy cost

worked_examples.final_answer: Cr: Expected [Ar] $4s^2 3d^4$, Actual [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$ (half-filled stability); Cu: Expected [Ar] $4s^2 3d^9$, Actual [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$ (fully-filled stability)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Half-filled ($d^5$) and fully-filled ($d^{10}$) d subshells provide extra stability that can overcome the energy cost of electron promotion.

worked_examples.title: Determining Unpaired Electrons in Exception Configurations
worked_examples.problem: How many unpaired electrons does chromium have in its actual configuration compared to the expected configuration?

step_by_step.step: Draw orbital diagram for expected Cr
Draw orbital diagram for actual Cr

step_by_step.explanation: Actual has 2 more unpaired electrons than expected
More unpaired electrons = stronger paramagnetism
step_by_step.calculation: Actual Cr is more paramagnetic than expected configuration would predict

step_by_step.explanation: Exception configuration affects observable magnetic properties
step_by_step.calculation: Experimental magnetic measurements confirm actual configuration

worked_examples.final_answer: Expected Cr: 4 unpaired electrons; Actual Cr: 6 unpaired electrons; actual configuration has 2 more unpaired electrons, making it more paramagnetic
worked_examples.concept_applied: Exception configurations often have different numbers of unpaired electrons than expected, affecting magnetic properties.

sections.key_concept: Beyond chromium and copper, several other transition metals have exception configurations, particularly in periods 5 and 6 where orbital energy differences are even smaller and relativistic effects become significant.

process_flow.description: Determine the element's group and period in the periodic table.
Follow standard filling order to write expected configuration.
Look for Groups 6, 10, 11, and other known exception groups.
Period 5 and 6 have more exceptions than period 4 due to smaller energy gaps.
Confirm actual configuration from reliable sources when uncertain.

classifications.value: More elements in period 5 show exceptions compared to period 4
classifications.behavior: 5s-4d energy gap smaller than 4s-3d, making more exceptions energetically favorable

examples.explanation: Pd: [Kr] $4d^{10}$; both 5s electrons promote to 4d for fully-filled stability; only element with no s electrons in ground state

classifications.value: Period 6 has exceptions influenced by f orbital effects and relativistic effects
characteristics: f orbital contraction affects d orbital energies
classifications.behavior: Relativistic effects become significant for heavy elements; 6s electrons more tightly bound

examples.explanation: Au: [Xe] $6s^1 5d^{10}$; relativistic effects contribute to stability of $d^{10}$ configuration
Pt: [Xe] $6s^1 5d^9$; nearly fully-filled d subshell with one s electron

classifications.value: f-block elements have numerous exceptions due to very small energy differences between 4f, 5d, and 6s orbitals
classifications.behavior: f-d-s energy differences very small; multiple configurations often close in energy

examples.explanation: Gd: [Xe] $6s^2 4f^7 5d^1$; half-filled 4f⁷ provides stability similar to Cr's 3d⁵

classifications.value: Many transition metals follow Aufbau principle without exceptions
classifications.behavior: These elements don't gain enough stability from promotion to overcome energy cost

examples.explanation: Ti: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^2$; promoting to $4s^1 3d^3$ doesn't achieve half-filled or fully-filled stability
Zn: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^{10}$; already has fully-filled 3d; no benefit from promotion

worked_examples.title: Identifying Period 5 Exceptions
worked_examples.problem: Which of the following period 5 elements have exception configurations: Y (Z=39), Nb (Z=41), Ru (Z=44), Cd (Z=48)?

step_by_step.explanation: Expected: [Kr] $5s^2 4d^1$; Actual: [Kr] $5s^2 4d^1$; no exception
Expected: [Kr] $5s^2 4d^3$; Actual: [Kr] $5s^1 4d^4$; exception
Expected: [Kr] $5s^2 4d^6$; Actual: [Kr] $5s^1 4d^7$; exception
Expected: [Kr] $5s^2 4d^{10}$; Actual: [Kr] $5s^2 4d^{10}$; no exception
Nb and Ru have exceptions; Y and Cd follow Aufbau

worked_examples.final_answer: Nb and Ru have exception configurations; Y and Cd follow expected Aufbau order
worked_examples.concept_applied: Period 5 has more exceptions than period 4 due to smaller 5s-4d energy gap.

worked_examples.title: Understanding Palladium's Unique Configuration
worked_examples.problem: Explain why palladium (Pd, Z=46) has the configuration [Kr] $4d^{10}$ with no 5s electrons, unlike other Group 10 elements.

step_by_step.explanation: Following Aufbau: [Kr] $5s^2 4d^8$
Actual: [Kr] $4d^{10}$; both 5s electrons promote to 4d
Fully-filled 4d¹⁰ provides maximum stability; no s electrons needed
Ni: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^8$; Pt: [Xe] $6s^1 5d^9$; Pd is unique with no s electrons
step_by_step.calculation: Pd is the only element with no s electrons in ground state

step_by_step.explanation: For Pd, stability from fully-filled 4d exceeds cost of emptying 5s completely
step_by_step.calculation: Exception most extreme for Pd among transition metals

worked_examples.final_answer: Palladium: [Kr] $4d^{10}$; both 5s electrons promote to 4d to achieve fully-filled d subshell; unique among elements for having no s electrons in ground state
worked_examples.concept_applied: Fully-filled d subshell stability can be so favorable that both s electrons promote to d orbitals.

sections.key_concept: When writing electron configurations for elements with exceptions, it's important to follow specific conventions and understand when to apply exception rules versus standard Aufbau order.

components.definition: Exception configurations are written using the same notation as standard configurations ($nl^x$ format), but with the actual electron distribution rather than the expected Aufbau order.
components.equation: Format: [Noble gas] $ns^1 (n-1)d^5$ for half-filled; [Noble gas] $ns^1 (n-1)d^{10}$ for fully-filled
components.interpretation: Always write the actual configuration, not the expected one; indicate exception when teaching or explaining.

image.alt: Diagram showing correct notation for Cr and Cu exception configurations
image.caption: Exception configurations written with actual electron distribution: Cr [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$, Cu [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$

principles.statement: Always write the actual electron configuration, not the expected Aufbau configuration, when the exception is known.
principles.application: For Cr, Cu, and other known exceptions, write the actual configuration even if it seems to violate Aufbau order.
principles.example: Write Cr as [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$, not [Ar] $4s^2 3d^4$, even though the latter follows Aufbau order
principles.analogy: Like writing someone's actual name, not the name you expected them to have.

principles.statement: Use noble gas notation for exception configurations just as for standard configurations.
principles.application: Abbreviate core electrons with noble gas in brackets; show valence electrons explicitly.
principles.analogy: Like using a nickname for the core electrons, showing only the important valence details.

principles.statement: Write orbitals in energy order (4s before 3d) even for exception configurations, not by principal quantum number.
principles.application: Maintain consistency with standard configuration notation; 4s written before 3d.
principles.analogy: Like organizing books by publication date even when some are special editions.

worked_examples.title: Writing Configuration for Molybdenum
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for molybdenum (Mo, Z=42), which is in the same group as chromium.

step_by_step.explanation: Mo is in period 5; previous noble gas is krypton (Kr, Z=36)
42 - 36 = 6 valence electrons beyond [Kr]
Mo is in Group 6 like Cr; expect $s^1 d^5$ configuration
Combine noble gas core with valence electrons
36 (Kr) + 1 (5s) + 5 (4d) = 42 electrons

worked_examples.final_answer: Molybdenum (Mo, Z=42): [Kr] $5s^1 4d^5$ (same exception pattern as chromium)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Elements in the same group often show similar exception patterns; Mo follows Cr's $s^1 d^5$ pattern.

worked_examples.title: Writing Configuration for Silver
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for silver (Ag, Z=47), which is in the same group as copper.

step_by_step.explanation: Ag is in period 5; previous noble gas is krypton (Kr, Z=36)
47 - 36 = 11 valence electrons beyond [Kr]
Ag is in Group 11 like Cu; expect $s^1 d^{10}$ configuration
Combine noble gas core with valence electrons
36 (Kr) + 1 (5s) + 10 (4d) = 47 electrons

worked_examples.final_answer: Silver (Ag, Z=47): [Kr] $5s^1 4d^{10}$ (same exception pattern as copper)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Elements in the same group often show similar exception patterns; Ag follows Cu's $s^1 d^{10}$ pattern.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstandings about electron configuration exceptions can lead to incorrect configurations and confusion about when exceptions apply. Clarifying these concepts builds accurate understanding.

classifications.type: Error: Writing Expected Instead of Actual Configuration
classifications.value: Writing the Aufbau-predicted configuration instead of the actual exception configuration
classifications.behavior: Memorize the common exceptions (Cr, Cu, Mo, Ag, Au); verify with reference when uncertain

examples.explanation: Wrong: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^4$; Correct: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$ (must memorize this exception)
Wrong: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^9$; Correct: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$ (must memorize this exception)

classifications.type: Error: Assuming All Transition Metals Have Exceptions
classifications.value: Thinking every transition metal has an exception configuration
classifications.behavior: Exceptions occur only for specific elements (mainly Groups 6, 10, 11); most transition metals follow Aufbau

examples.explanation: Wrong: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^3$; Correct: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^2$ (no exception for Ti)
Wrong: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$; Correct: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^{10}$ (no exception for Zn)

classifications.type: Error: Incorrect Electron Count in Exceptions
classifications.value: Changing total electron count when writing exception configurations
characteristics: Writing Cr with 23 or 25 electrons instead of 24
Writing Cu with 28 or 30 electrons instead of 29
Adding or removing electrons during promotion
classifications.behavior: Promotion moves electrons between orbitals; total electron count must equal atomic number

examples.explanation: Wrong: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$ with 23e⁻; Correct: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$ with 24e⁻ (18+1+5=24)

classifications.value: Thinking exceptions are random or arbitrary rather than based on stability principles
characteristics: Cannot predict exceptions in period 5 or 6
classifications.behavior: Understand: half-filled ($d^5$) and fully-filled ($d^{10}$) d subshells provide extra stability through exchange energy and symmetry

examples.explanation: Wrong: random exception; Correct: $d^5$ half-filled maximizes exchange energy stability
Wrong: random exception; Correct: $d^{10}$ fully-filled maximizes symmetry and minimizes repulsion

classifications.type: Error: Applying Exception Rules to Ions Incorrectly
classifications.value: Not knowing how exception configurations affect ion formation
characteristics: Removing d electrons before s electrons for exception elements
Confusing neutral atom vs. ion configurations
classifications.behavior: For ions, remove from highest n first (s before d) even for exception elements; Cr²⁺: [Ar] $3d^4$ not [Ar] $4s^1 3d^3$

examples.explanation: Wrong: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^3$; Correct: [Ar] $3d^4$ (remove 4s electron first)
Wrong: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^9$; Correct: [Ar] $3d^{10}$ (remove 4s electron first)

sections.introduction: Test your understanding of electron configuration exceptions with these practice problems. Attempt each before reviewing the solution.

key_concept.definition: Practice reinforces understanding of when exceptions occur, how to write exception configurations, and why they happen.
key_concept.context: Focus on memorizing common exceptions and understanding the stability principles behind them.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for chromium (Cr, Z=24) and explain why it differs from the expected Aufbau configuration.

step_by_step.explanation: Expected: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^4$
Half-filled 3d⁵ subshell provides extra stability through exchange energy and symmetric distribution

worked_examples.final_answer: Cr: [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$; exception occurs because half-filled 3d⁵ maximizes exchange energy stability
worked_examples.concept_applied: Half-filled d subshells ($d^5$) are particularly stable.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Write the electron configuration for silver (Ag, Z=47).

step_by_step.explanation: Previous noble gas is Kr (Z=36)
Ag is in same group as Cu; expect $s^1 d^{10}$
Combine core and valence

worked_examples.concept_applied: Group 11 elements (Cu, Ag, Au) show $s^1 d^{10}$ exception pattern.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: Does titanium (Ti, Z=22) have an exception configuration? Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: Following Aufbau: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^2$
Would give 3d³, not half-filled or fully-filled
No exception; Ti follows Aufbau order

worked_examples.final_answer: Titanium does NOT have an exception configuration; it follows expected Aufbau order: [Ar] $4s^2 3d^2$
worked_examples.concept_applied: Not all transition metals have exceptions; only those that gain stability from half-filled or fully-filled d subshells.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: How many unpaired electrons does copper have in its actual configuration?

step_by_step.explanation: One unpaired electron in 4s orbital

worked_examples.final_answer: Copper has 1 unpaired electron (in 4s orbital); paramagnetic
worked_examples.concept_applied: Exception configurations affect unpaired electron count and magnetic properties.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: Explain why palladium (Pd, Z=46) has the unique configuration [Kr] $4d^{10}$ with no 5s electrons.

step_by_step.explanation: Following Aufbau: [Kr] $5s^2 4d^8$
Actual: [Kr] $4d^{10}$; both 5s electrons promote to 4d
Fully-filled 4d¹⁰ provides maximum stability; energy gain exceeds cost of emptying 5s
step_by_step.calculation: Most extreme exception among transition metals

step_by_step.explanation: Pd is the only element with no s electrons in ground state

worked_examples.final_answer: Palladium: [Kr] $4d^{10}$; both 5s electrons promote to 4d for fully-filled stability; unique for having no s electrons in ground state
worked_examples.concept_applied: Fully-filled d subshell stability can be so favorable that both s electrons promote to d orbitals.

summary: Electron configuration exceptions occur when half-filled ($d^5$) or fully-filled ($d^{10}$) d subshells provide extra stability through exchange energy and symmetric electron distribution. Classic examples: Cr (Z=24): [Ar] $4s^1 3d^5$ not [Ar] $4s^2 3d^4$ (half-filled stability); Cu (Z=29): [Ar] $4s^1 3d^{10}$ not [Ar] $4s^2 3d^9$ (fully-filled stability). Other exceptions: Mo (period 5, same as Cr), Ag, Au (period 5-6, same as Cu), Pd ([Kr] $4d^{10}$ with no 5s electrons, unique). Exceptions occur because 4s-3d energy gap is small (~2-3 eV), making electron promotion energetically favorable when stability is gained. Exchange energy from parallel spins in half-filled subshells and reduced electron repulsion in fully-filled subshells provide stabilization. Most transition metals follow Aufbau without exceptions (Sc, Ti, V, Zn, etc.). Period 5 has more exceptions than period 4 due to smaller 5s-4d gap. Common errors include: writing expected instead of actual configurations, assuming all transition metals have exceptions, incorrect electron counts, misunderstanding why exceptions occur, and misapplying exception rules to ions. For ions, remove from highest n first (s before d) even for exception elements. Writing conventions: use noble gas notation, write in energy order (4s before 3d), always write actual not expected configuration. Mastery requires memorizing common exceptions (Cr, Cu, Mo, Ag, Au, Pd) and understanding stability principles behind them.
